headline: How an Underground Fridge Keeps Food Cool Without Electricity
meta_description: A Dutch-designed Ground Fridge uses the earth's stable temperature to store food with minimal electricity, reviving an ancient cooling method for modern times.
summary_short: Buried in a London garden, a clever underground fridge keeps produce cool during heatwaves—using no mains electricity and storing as much as a dozen family fridges.
body:
High on a hill in north London, a fridge lies half-buried in a flowerbed. Step through its door, as tall as a human and half as broad, and you descend into a small underground room lined with shelves, blissfully cool even on scorching summer days.

This is the Ground Fridge—a modern invention inspired by centuries-old ice houses and earth cellars. Several feet below ground, insulated by soil and plants, temperatures remain stable and cool without needing traditional refrigeration. A solar-powered ventilation system draws in cool night air to replace the warmth of the day.

The Dutch designer Floris Schoonderbeek created this ingenious device, and its first UK installation sits at OmVed Gardens, an extraordinary two-acre urban farm in Highgate. There, fermentation specialist Josefine Marchandise runs a regenerative kitchen that harvests about 20 varieties of vegetables from the surrounding terraces. She's become a devoted fan.

"The temperature stays between 8 and 15 degrees, even during heatwaves," Marchandise explains. "It's lovely to come out into the garden to fetch something from the fridge, rather than just staying in the kitchen."

At around £12,000 plus VAT, the Ground Fridge isn't cheap—but it has the storage capacity of a dozen full-size family fridges while using almost no mains electricity. As visitors to OmVed Gardens discover it, they ask the same question: "Why isn't this everywhere?"
